subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Confuses part:part ratio with part:whole ratio
explanation: When asked for ratio of one group to the total, student gives ratio of one group to the other group instead.
why_students_think_this: The word 'ratio' is taught with part:part examples (boys to girls). Part:whole is a different structure students haven't internalised.
trig_impact: sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse is a part:whole ratio. Students with this gap will set it up as opposite:adjacent instead.
root_cause: No distinction between part:part and part:whole ratio structures

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Thinks ratio changes when triangle size changes
explanation: Student believes that if a triangle is scaled up, the ratio of two sides changes because the sides are now larger numbers.
why_students_think_this: Absolute size feels like it should matter — a bigger triangle 'should' give a bigger ratio. Students conflate the ratio with the side length.
trig_impact: This is the core insight behind trig. If the ratio changes with size, then sin 30° is not a fixed number — the entire concept of trig ratios collapses.
root_cause: No understanding of ratio as scale-invariant. Multiplicative reasoning not yet developed.

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Inverts the ratio — puts wrong term in numerator
explanation: When asked for A/B, student gives B/A instead. Consistent inversion suggests no understanding of which quantity is being expressed 'per unit' of the other.
why_students_think_this: Fractions feel arbitrary in direction. Students pick the larger number for the numerator, or reverse the order of the question.
trig_impact: Will consistently produce 1/sin θ instead of sin θ, or use adjacent/hypotenuse when asked for opposite/hypotenuse.
root_cause: No conceptual model for what a fraction represents as a directed relationship

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Confuses similar triangles with congruent triangles
explanation: Student thinks 'similar' means the triangles are identical — same angles AND same sides. Does not understand that similar means proportional, not equal.
why_students_think_this: In everyday language, 'similar' means 'almost the same'. Mathematical similarity is a technical term with a precise meaning students haven't internalised.
trig_impact: Student will not understand why sin 30° is the same in a small and a large triangle. Will think trig ratios are only valid for specific triangle sizes.
root_cause: Everyday language meaning of 'similar' overrides mathematical meaning

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Thinks angle information alone is insufficient to prove similarity
explanation: Student believes you need side lengths to prove similarity, not just angles. Does not know the AA (Angle-Angle) similarity condition.
why_students_think_this: Congruence requires side information (SSS, SAS), so students overgeneralise — thinking similarity also needs sides.
trig_impact: Student cannot reason that all 30-60-90 triangles are similar to each other, which is the justification for trig ratios being constant.

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Applies Pythagoras formula without understanding which side is the hypotenuse
explanation: Student knows a² + b² = c² but cannot reliably identify which side is c (the hypotenuse) from a problem description or diagram. May subtract squares in the wrong order.
why_students_think_this: Taught the formula with labelled diagrams where c is always obvious. When the problem gives a real-world context, the student cannot map it to the formula.
trig_impact: Will miscalculate the hypotenuse in trig problems. In sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse, using the wrong side as hypotenuse gives a ratio > 1, which is impossible.
root_cause: Formula memorised without understanding that hypotenuse = longest side = side opposite the right angle

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Treats Pythagoras as addition/subtraction of sides, not squares
explanation: Student uses a + b = c or c - a = b instead of a² + b² = c². Squaring is omitted entirely.
why_students_think_this: Addition is the default operation for combining quantities. Squaring feels like an arbitrary extra step students drop under pressure.
trig_impact: Will produce wrong side lengths for all trig ratio calculations. A 3-4-5 triangle becomes a 3-4-7 triangle, making sin θ = 3/7 instead of 3/5.
root_cause: No understanding of why squares are involved — theorem taught as formula, not as area relationship

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Cannot reliably evaluate or set up a fraction from a ratio description
explanation: When told 'opposite divided by hypotenuse', student inverts the fraction or substitutes the wrong sides. The fraction structure of trig ratios creates a consistent operation error.
why_students_think_this: Fractions as division are not fluent. Students see 'opposite/hypotenuse' as two separate numbers, not as a single quantity with a direction.
trig_impact: Direct and immediate. Every trig calculation requires forming a fraction. If the fraction is inverted or uses wrong sides, every answer is wrong.
root_cause: Fraction as division not internalised — numerator/denominator feels arbitrary

subject: trig_prerequisites
misconception_name: Cannot compare or order fractions with different denominators
explanation: Student cannot determine which of two fractions is larger without a calculator, or uses whole-number reasoning (larger numerator = larger fraction).
why_students_think_this: Whole number intuition: 5 > 3, so 5/12 > 3/8 feels right for the wrong reason. Students don't convert to common denominators or decimals fluently.
trig_impact: Student cannot reason about whether sin θ is large or small for a given angle, or compare trig values without a table. Limits application problem solving.
root_cause: Fraction comparison taught procedurally — students apply cross-multiplication mechanically without understanding
